result:
# The Weight of Ordinary Moments

I've been thinking lately about how we measure our lives—not in the grand gestures or milestone moments we post about, but in the strange, quiet accumulations that nobody sees.

Yesterday I watched an old man at a coffee shop spend forty-five minutes reading a newspaper. An actual paper newspaper, the kind that leaves ink on your fingertips. He turned each page with this deliberate care, like he was handling something precious. And maybe he was. Maybe that ritual—the walk to the shop, the same order he's probably made for decades, the familiar rustle of newsprint—maybe that's the architecture of his entire day. Maybe dismantling it would collapse something essential.

We're so obsessed with optimization now. Every minute should be leveraged, every hobby should become a side hustle, every walk should hit a step count. But that old man wasn't optimizing anything. He was just *being* somewhere, doing something that mattered to him for reasons that don't need to translate into productivity metrics.

I wonder sometimes if we've lost the art of the ordinary.

---

There's a street in my mental geography—not a real street, but an amalgamation of all the streets I've ever loved. It has the maple trees from my childhood neighborhood, the kind that turned impossible shades of orange in October. It has the uneven sidewalks of that college town where I first felt like an adult, where the concrete had been lifted by roots and you learned to watch your step without thinking about it. It has the particular slant of late afternoon light from somewhere I can't quite place anymore.

We carry these composite places inside us. They're not memories exactly—they're more like emotional coordinates. Points on a map of who we've been.

I think that's why going back to places from our past is always slightly disappointing. The real place can never match the mythologized version we've been carrying. The restaurant is smaller than we remembered. The park where we had our first kiss is just a park. The magic wasn't in the location—it was in us, in that specific configuration of who we were at that moment, which we can never reassemble.

---

Here's something I've never fully understood: why do we apologize for crying?

Someone starts to tear up and immediately says "sorry, sorry" while wiping their eyes, as if they've committed some social transgression. As if feeling things deeply enough to produce a physical response is somehow rude. We treat tears like a malfunction rather than a feature. Like our bodies have betrayed us by doing exactly what they evolved to do.

I've seen people apologize for crying at funerals. *At funerals.* The one place where tears are not just appropriate but almost required, and still we feel we need to excuse ourselves.

Maybe it's because crying is honest in a way that makes us vulnerable. It's the body saying what the mouth won't. And we've built entire social structures around the careful management of what we reveal. Crying crashes through all that. It says: this matters to me, and I cannot hide how much.

---

I've been reading about trees lately—specifically about how forests communicate. Through fungal networks in the soil, trees share nutrients. A mother tree will send carbon to her seedlings through these underground pathways. When one tree is dying, it sometimes dumps its resources into the network for others to use. They're not individual organisms competing for resources so much as nodes in a larger system, bound together in ways we're only beginning to understand.

There's something both comforting and unsettling about this. Comforting because it suggests connection is fundamental, written into the operating system of life itself. Unsettling because it challenges our whole mythology of rugged individualism, survival of the fittest, every organism for itself.

What if competition is actually the exception? What if the rule, the deeper truth, is interdependence?

---

I used to keep a list of things I wanted to do before I died. The usual stuff: places to visit, experiences to have, achievements to unlock. But I abandoned the list a few years ago. Not because I accomplished everything on it—I didn't—but because I realized the list had become a kind of weight. Every day I wasn't checking something off felt like a day wasted. The list had turned the future into a series of obligations rather than possibilities.

Now I try to approach things differently. Less "what do I want to accomplish" and more "what do I want to practice." What qualities do I want to develop? What kind of attention do I want to pay to my life?

It's a subtle shift, but it matters. Accomplishments are endpoints; practices are ongoing. You can fail at a goal, but you can always return to a practice. You just begin again, as many times as necessary.

---

The light is changing as I write this. That's something I might not have noticed a few years ago—the specific quality of afternoon light transitioning toward evening. The way shadows lengthen and colors deepen. It happens every day, this slow performance, and mostly we miss it because we're looking at screens or lost in thought or rushing somewhere.

But today I'm watching. And there's something in the watching itself that feels important. Not productive, not useful, not optimized. Just present.

Maybe that's enough. Maybe that's more than enough.

Maybe the old man with his newspaper knows something we've forgotten. That being somewhere fully, doing something simply, paying attention to what's right in front of us—maybe that's not a waste of time. Maybe that's the whole point.

The light keeps changing. The day keeps ending. And tomorrow there will be another chance to notice, or not notice, the ordinary extraordinary fact of being alive.

---

*That's what I wanted to write about today. Thanks for the space to wander.*
